unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9: Amazing Arts — the final unit of Year 6! In this unit you will explore how art, music, and creative expression can transform communities and inspire change. You will read about children who turn broken objects into beautiful instruments, empty walls into colourful murals, and dusty valleys into thriving green spaces. Through these stories and activities, you will sharpen your English skills and discover the power of creativity.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Indefinite Pronouns, Adjective Order, Present Perfect review).
Read the Story file, “The Children of Wind Valley”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
Arts and Expression
transform — to change something into a different form or appearance.
cultivate — to prepare and care for something so it can grow.
flourished — grew or developed in a healthy, successful way.
landscape — the visible features of an area of land.
cursive — a style of writing where the letters are connected together.
pluralistic — having many different ideas, cultures, or groups living together.
Environment and Change
deforestation — the process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest.
preserve — to keep something in its original state so it is not damaged.
sacrifice — giving up something important for the sake of something else.
reform — to make changes to something to improve it.
activism — the act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause.
turbine — a machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy.
People and Society
collide — to hit something with a lot of force; to come into conflict.
relocate — to move to a different place.
overthrow — to remove a leader or government from power.
disgrace — a feeling of shame or loss of respect.
altitude — the height of something above sea level.
access — the ability to enter or use something.
Reading: The Mural Makers of Mogadishu
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.
In many cities around the world, art has the power to transform empty spaces into places of hope and beauty. One of the most exciting examples of this comes from communities where young people pick up paintbrushes instead of waiting for change to arrive. They decide that everyone deserves to see colour and creativity in their daily lives — and they make it happen themselves.
In a neighbourhood on the outskirts of a large East African city, a group of teenagers noticed that the walls along the main road were cracked, grey, and covered in dust. Nothing about the street felt welcoming. Families walked past quickly, and children had nowhere special to play. The teenagers decided to change that. They formed a group called the Mural Makers.
The Mural Makers began by collecting leftover paint from local shops and workshops. Someone donated old brushes. Anyone who wanted to help was welcome, and soon the group grew to more than thirty young artists. They did not need expensive equipment or access to a fancy studio. All they needed was a wall, a plan, and the commitment to finish what they started.
Their first project was a mural on the side of a community hall. Samira, the group’s leader, sketched the design in her notebook using neat cursive handwriting. The mural showed a landscape of green hills, a bright sun, and children planting trees together. “We want people to see what this place could look like,” Samira explained. “Art helps us imagine a better future.”
The teenagers learned to organise their adjectives carefully when describing their supplies. They asked for “a large rectangular wooden board” to mix paint on, and they described their finished mural as “a beautiful tall colourful East African scene.” This kind of careful adjective order made their English writing sound natural and polished.
Slowly, the neighbourhood began to change. Where there had been bare walls, there were now bright images of animals, flowers, and famous East African landscapes. An old man who had lived on the street for decades said, “Everything looks different now. I feel proud of where I live.”
But the Mural Makers did not stop at painting. They also started a campaign to preserve the few remaining trees on their street. Deforestation in the surrounding area had removed much of the shade, and the altitude of the neighbourhood meant hot winds blew dust through the streets. The group planted thirty new seedlings, each one painted with a small number in bright green.
One member of the group, Ishmael, was interested in music. He began building simple instruments from recycled materials — a drum from an old plastic container, a guitar from scrap wood and wire. “Something beautiful can come from anything,” he told the younger children who gathered to watch. He taught them simple rhythms and songs about caring for the environment.
Word about the Mural Makers spread to other parts of the city. A local newspaper wrote about their work. A school in another district invited them to paint a mural in the playground. Somebody from a community radio station interviewed Samira. “Art is activism,” she said. “When we paint, we are saying that this place matters. When we plant a tree, we are saying the future matters.”
Not everyone understood at first. Some adults thought the murals were a waste of time. One shopkeeper said, “Paint does not feed families.” But when more visitors started coming to the neighbourhood to see the murals, and when a small market opened next to the community hall, even the doubters began to see the value. Art had not only made the street beautiful — it had helped the local economy flourish.
The Mural Makers have now completed twelve murals, planted over a hundred trees, and built a small music workshop where children learn to play recycled instruments every Saturday. Their motto, painted in large cursive letters on the community hall, reads: “Nothing is wasted. Everything is art. Everyone belongs.”
Samira says she hopes the project will transform not just their neighbourhood but also how people think about young artists. “We have shown that anyone can create change,” she said. “You do not need permission to make the world more beautiful. You just need to start.”
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What problem did the teenagers notice about their street at the beginning of the passage?
How did the Mural Makers get the materials they needed to paint? Mention at least two ways.
What did Samira’s first mural show, and what message did she want it to send?
How did Ishmael contribute to the group in a different way from the other members?
The passage says “Art is activism.” In your own words, explain what Samira meant by this.
How did the murals help the local economy? Find the evidence in the text.
What is the Mural Makers’ motto? Why do you think they chose those three sentences?
Find three indefinite pronouns used in the passage and write the full sentence for each one.
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
The shopkeeper said, “Paint does not feed families.” Do you agree or disagree? Give your reasons.
Samira says, “You do not need permission to make the world more beautiful.” What does this mean for young people in your community?
If you could paint a mural on a wall in your town or neighbourhood, what would you paint and why?
How is what the Mural Makers did similar to what the children do in this unit’s story, “The Children of Wind Valley”?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
transform
commitment
preserve
deforestation
activism
flourish
Grammar Focus: Indefinite Pronouns
This section introduces a key grammar topic for Unit 9. You will study it in more detail in your Grammar companion file, but here is a summary to get you started.
What are indefinite pronouns?
Indefinite pronouns are words that refer to people or things without saying exactly who or what they are. They are very useful when you want to talk about people or things in a general way.
There are four main groups:
some- words (for positive statements): someone, somebody, something, somewhere.
any- words (for questions and negatives): anyone, anybody, anything, anywhere.
every- words (for all people or things): everyone, everybody, everything, everywhere.
no- words (for zero, none): no one, nobody, nothing, nowhere.
Important grammar rules
Indefinite pronouns that end in -one, -body, or -thing are always singular. This means they take a singular verb:
Everyone is welcome. (not “Everyone are welcome.”)
Nobody knows the answer. (not “Nobody know the answer.”)
Something smells wonderful in the kitchen.
However, in modern English, we often use they/their/them to refer back to an indefinite pronoun when we do not know the person’s gender:
Someone left their bag in the classroom.
Everyone should bring their own notebook.
Using indefinite pronouns in different sentence types
Look at how we choose the right pronoun depending on the type of sentence:
Positive statement: Someone donated old paintbrushes to the group.
Question: Did anyone bring extra paint today?
Negative meaning: No one wanted to miss the mural unveiling.
Universal: Everything in the workshop was made from recycled materials.
Indefinite pronouns in the reading passage
Go back to the reading passage and find at least five indefinite pronouns. For each one, write:
The full sentence it appears in.
Whether it is a some-, any-, every-, or no- pronoun.
Whether it refers to a person, a thing, or a place.
Quick Practice
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun to complete each sentence:
______ (Someone / Anyone) left a beautiful drawing on the teacher’s desk.
Is there ______ (something / anything) we can use to clean these brushes?
______ (Everyone / No one) in the class enjoyed the art exhibition.
There is ______ (nothing / anything) wrong with making mistakes — that is how we learn.
She looked ______ (everywhere / somewhere) for her paintbrush but could not find it.
______ (Nobody / Somebody) told me the concert was cancelled, so I went anyway.
Answers: 1. Someone   2. anything   3. Everyone   4. nothing   5. everywhere   6. Nobody
Grammar Focus: Adjective Order
When we use more than one adjective before a noun, English has a natural order. If we get the order wrong, the sentence sounds strange, even if the grammar is technically correct.
The standard adjective order
Remember the order using the letters OSASCOMP:
O — Opinion (beautiful, ugly, amazing, terrible)
S — Size (big, small, tiny, enormous, tall)
A — Age (old, new, young, ancient)
S — Shape (round, square, flat, rectangular)
C — Colour (red, blue, green, bright, dark)
O — Origin (Somali, East African, British, Chinese)
M — Material (wooden, metal, plastic, stone)
P — Purpose (cooking, sleeping, painting, writing)
Examples from the passage
Notice how adjectives are ordered naturally in these phrases from the reading:
“a large rectangular wooden board” — size (large) + shape (rectangular) + material (wooden)
“a beautiful tall colourful East African scene” — opinion (beautiful) + size (tall) + colour (colourful) + origin (East African)
More examples
“a small old round table” — size + age + shape
“a lovely bright green Somali cloth” — opinion + colour + origin
“a tiny new circular metal tray” — size + age + shape + material
A common mistake
Mistake: “a wooden large rectangular board” ✔ This sounds wrong because material (wooden) comes before size (large).
Fix: “a large rectangular wooden board” ✔ Size → shape → material.
Quick Practice
Put the adjectives in the correct order before the noun:
a ______ ______ ______ drum (old / big / plastic)
a ______ ______ ______ mural (East African / colourful / beautiful)
______ ______ ______ paintbrushes (wooden / new / small)
a ______ ______ ______ building (stone / ancient / enormous)
Answers: 1. a big old plastic drum   2. a beautiful colourful East African mural   3. small new wooden paintbrushes   4. an enormous ancient stone building
Grammar Practice
These exercises bring together indefinite pronouns and adjective order. Complete them in your notebook, then check your answers at the bottom or ask your AI tutor.
Exercise A: Indefinite Pronouns in Context
Complete each sentence with the best indefinite pronoun from the box.
(someone, anyone, everyone, no one, something, anything, nothing, everything, somewhere, nowhere)
______ brought recycled bottles to the art class — we do not know who it was.
Is there ______ in this room who can play a musical instrument?
After the clean-up, ______ was left on the ground — it was spotless!
______ in our school believes that creativity is important.
I looked ______ for my sketchbook, but I cannot find it.
We need ______ strong to help carry the paint cans to the hall.
______ in her art portfolio was made from recycled materials.
There was ______ to sit in the crowded gallery, so we stood near the back.
Answers: 1. Someone   2. anyone   3. nothing   4. Everyone   5. everywhere   6. someone   7. Everything   8. nowhere
Exercise B: Adjective Order Challenge
Rewrite each phrase with the adjectives in the correct order:
a (Somali / traditional / beautiful) rug
(recycled / small / three) drums
a (rectangular / new / large) canvas
an (stone / amazing / ancient / East African) carving
some (metal / shiny / tiny) beads
Answers: 1. a beautiful traditional Somali rug   2. three small recycled drums   3. a large new rectangular canvas   4. an amazing ancient East African stone carving   5. some tiny shiny metal beads
Exercise C: Present Perfect Review
The present perfect tense (have/has + past participle) is used throughout the story and reading passage to show actions that started in the past and connect to the present. Complete these sentences:
The Mural Makers ______ (complete) twelve murals so far.
Samira ______ (inspire) many young artists in her community.
We ______ (plant) over a hundred trees this year.
Isaac ______ (build) instruments from recycled materials.
The children ______ (show) that anyone can create change.
I ______ (never / visit) an art gallery before.
Answers: 1. have completed   2. has inspired   3. have planted   4. has built   5. have shown   6. have never visited
Exercise D: Mixed Grammar — Write Your Own
Write six sentences about your community or school. Each sentence must include:
Two sentences with an indefinite pronoun (e.g., “Everyone in my neighbourhood enjoys…”).
Two sentences with two or more adjectives in the correct order (e.g., “We have a large old wooden gate.”).
Two sentences using the present perfect tense (e.g., “Our school has planted new trees.”).

readings.title: Amazing Arts: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 9: Amazing Arts — the final unit of Year 6! In this unit you will explore how art, music, and creative expression can transform communities and inspire change. You will read about children who turn broken objects into beautiful instruments, empty walls into colourful murals, and dusty valleys into thriving green spaces. Through these stories and activities, you will sharpen your English skills and discover the power of creativity.
This unit connects reading, grammar, vocabulary, writing and speaking into one rich topic. By the end, you will be able to talk confidently about art, the environment, and community projects — all while using more advanced grammar like indefinite pronouns and adjective order.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a creative-arts passage and discuss its ideas in English.
Use indefinite pronouns (someone, anyone, everyone, nothing, etc.) correctly in sentences.
Order multiple adjectives properly before a noun (opinion → size → age → colour → origin → material).
Review the present perfect tense to describe what has already been done.
Write a detailed art project description using precise vocabulary.
Present your creative ideas clearly in a short spoken presentation.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Indefinite Pronouns, Adjective Order, Present Perfect review).
Read the Story file, “The Children of Wind Valley”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
transform — to change something into a different form or appearance.
cultivate — to prepare and care for something so it can grow.
flourished — grew or developed in a healthy, successful way.
landscape — the visible features of an area of land.
cursive — a style of writing where the letters are connected together.
pluralistic — having many different ideas, cultures, or groups living together.
deforestation — the process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest.
preserve — to keep something in its original state so it is not damaged.
sacrifice — giving up something important for the sake of something else.
reform — to make changes to something to improve it.

readings.title: Amazing Arts: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
activism — the act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause.
turbine — a machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy.
collide — to hit something with a lot of force; to come into conflict.
relocate — to move to a different place.
overthrow — to remove a leader or government from power.
disgrace — a feeling of shame or loss of respect.
altitude — the height of something above sea level.
access — the ability to enter or use something.
Reading: The Mural Makers of Mogadishu
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.
In many cities around the world, art has the power to transform empty spaces into places of hope and beauty. One of the most exciting examples of this comes from communities where young people pick up paintbrushes instead of waiting for change to arrive. They decide that everyone deserves to see colour and creativity in their daily lives — and they make it happen themselves.
In a neighbourhood on the outskirts of a large East African city, a group of teenagers noticed that the walls along the main road were cracked, grey, and covered in dust. Nothing about the street felt welcoming. Families walked past quickly, and children had nowhere special to play. The teenagers decided to change that. They formed a group called the Mural Makers.
The Mural Makers began by collecting leftover paint from local shops and workshops. Someone donated old brushes. Anyone who wanted to help was welcome, and soon the group grew to more than thirty young artists. They did not need expensive equipment or access to a fancy studio. All they needed was a wall, a plan, and the commitment to finish what they started.
Their first project was a mural on the side of a community hall. Samira, the group’s leader, sketched the design in her notebook using neat cursive handwriting. The mural showed a landscape of green hills, a bright sun, and children planting trees together. “We want people to see what this place could look like,” Samira explained. “Art helps us imagine a better future.”
The teenagers learned to organise their adjectives carefully when describing their supplies. They asked for “a large rectangular wooden board” to mix paint on, and they described their finished mural as “a beautiful tall colourful East African scene.” This kind of careful adjective order made their English writing sound natural and polished.
Slowly, the neighbourhood began to change. Where there had been bare walls, there were now bright images of animals, flowers, and famous East African landscapes. An old man who had lived on the street for decades said, “Everything looks different now. I feel proud of where I live.”

readings.title: The Children of Wind Valley, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The village of Lusinga rested in a wide valley surrounded by high ridges and evergreen trees. It was once famous for its cool climate, wildflowers, and strong winds that whispered through the hills. But recently, the land had begun to suffer.
"There used to be so many trees here," said Sarah, age 13, as she climbed a slope with her twin brother, Ishmael. "Now look at this mess."
The mountain landscape was dry. Broken stumps replaced old forest trails. Empty cans and plastic hung on fallen branches. People had cleared much of the forest for firewood and farming. It was called deforestation — and it had changed everything.
"Even the wind feels wrong," said Ishmael. "It's hotter now. The streams have dried. And the peppermint plants are gone."
They sat on a rock overlooking the valley.
"We need to do something," Sarah said.
At school the next day, their science teacher announced a project.
"You will design a plan to help the environment," she said. "This region has suffered. Now it's your turn to help it recover."
"What can we do?" asked a boy.
"That's for you to analyze," she replied. "It may involve new tools, new rules — or even a new way of thinking."
The students were excited. Sarah and Ishmael formed a group with their classmates Sophia and Adam. They named their group The Children of Wind Valley.
The first thing they did was research.
"We've already lost 60% of our forest," said Sophia, reading aloud. "Many animals have relocated. The soil is dry. And we've had more floods this year than ever before."
Isaac pointed at a graph. "Temperatures have flourished, too. This valley used to be cooler. Now it's close to the subtropics."
Adam nodded. "My grandmother says the wind used to sing. Now it screams."
They decided on a goal: to preserve what was left and replant what had been lost.
They made posters and maps. Adam wrote all their titles in neat cursive. One said:
"Wind Valley is our heritage.
Nature is not just space — it's memory."
They visited a local farm with a wind turbine. The farmer explained how it worked.
"The turbine collects the wind's power and turns it into electricity," he said.
"Could we build a small one at school?" Ishmael asked.
"With the right support," the farmer replied. "You'd need planning, commitment, and money."
The team met under the big fig tree after school. Sophia held up a drawing.
"Imagine it," she said. "A small wind farm. A tree nursery. A learning centre."
"It'll take real sacrifice," said Sarah. "Time, energy... Maybe people won't like it."

readings.title: The Children of Wind Valley, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"But it could be a model for other schools," said Adam. "A thriving space for nature and learning."
They called it the Ridge Replanting Project.
They wrote a proposal using the present perfect:
"We have measured changes in temperature and soil quality.
We have mapped the affected zones.
We have spoken to local farmers and elders."
"We will plant 100 trees.
We will build a wind model.
We will create a new environmental club."
They used clear adjective order too: "a small wooden solar-powered nursery," and "a wide circular concrete platform."
"This is just school talk," said a shopkeeper. "It's not real."
One man shouted, "Stop blaming us for cutting trees! You want us to starve?"
Some accused them of trying to overthrow tradition.
"That's not our goal," Sarah said in a public meeting. "We're not blaming. We're planting. Not to destroy the past — but to protect the future."
The district officer, Ms. Sophia, came to their school.
"I've read your proposal," she said. "It's detailed. And powerful. Especially your section on reform."
She showed them her notes. "You've used real data. Emotional writing. And vision."
"Will we get support?" Sarah asked.
"You've earned a pilot grant," she said. "Enough for 50 seedlings, and a model turbine. But you must monitor everything — and report weekly."
They were shocked. "We did it!" said Sophia.
That weekend, they began the planting. Students dug holes. Elders helped carry compost. A local artist painted signs:
"This ridge is a pluralistic place — where animals, trees, and children all belong."
They cleaned an old storehouse and turned it into a learning room.
They called it The Green Room.
Inside, Sarah taped a sign on the wall:
"We have sacrificed comfort for hope. We have collided with doubt.
But this is our space of transformation."
By the end of the term, 47 trees had survived. Bees returned. Butterflies fluttered near the new flowers. Some peppermint bushes sprouted again.
The school held an open day. Visitors toured the ridge and the Green Room. Many said it was beautiful.
Occasionally, someone called it "just a school project."
But then came a letter — from a school in Rwanda.
"We heard about your work.
Could you send us your report?
We want to start something too."
In the final assembly, the principal read from their original submission.
"We believe in environmental action rooted in education and respect.
The wind has shaped our stories. Now we shape the wind."
Outside, the wind turned again — this time softer. The trees moved gently, and the ridge whispered.
"They said we couldn't change anything."

grammar.title: Indefinite Pronouns — The Basics
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Indefinite pronouns are words that replace nouns without saying exactly who or what they refer to. They let us talk about people, things, or places in a general way. For example, instead of naming a specific person, you can say someone. Instead of listing every object, you can say everything. This is very useful in both speaking and writing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Indefinite pronouns are words that replace nouns without saying exactly who or what they refer to. They let us talk about people, things, or places in a general way. For example, instead of naming a specific person, you can say someone. Instead of listing every object, you can say everything. This is very useful in both speaking and writing.
The four groups
Indefinite pronouns come in four families. Each family has words for people, things, and places:
When to use each group
some- words are used in positive statements and offers: "Someone left a gift on the desk." / "Would you like something to drink?"
any- words are used in questions and negative sentences: "Does anyone have a pencil?" / "I do not have anything to wear."
every- words mean ALL people, things, or places: "Everyone enjoyed the show." / "She looked everywhere."
no- words mean ZERO — none at all: "Nobody was late today." / "There is nothing left to eat."
The singular verb rule
All indefinite pronouns that end in -one, -body, or -thing are singular. They take a singular verb:
Everyone is here. (not "Everyone are here.")
Nobody wants to leave. (not "Nobody want to leave.")
Something smells wonderful.
Nothing has changed.
A common mistake
Mistake: "Everyone are happy about the mural." ✗
Fix: "Everyone is happy about the mural." ✓
Remember: even though "everyone" means many people, the word itself is singular.
Another common mistake is confusing someone and anyone:
Mistake: "Anyone left their jacket in the classroom." ✗ (This sounds like a question about any random person.)
Fix: "Someone left their jacket in the classroom." ✓ (A specific but unknown person.)
Memory tip
Think of the word "indefinite" as "not definite" — not specific. These pronouns help you when you do not need to name exactly who or what. Some = a positive unknown. Any = a question or negative unknown. Every = all of them. No = none of them.
Practice 1
Fill in the blank with the correct indefinite pronoun from the box:
(someone, anyone, everyone, no one, something, anything, nothing, everything)
______ left a beautiful painting on the teacher's desk. We do not know who.
Is ______ interested in joining the art club?
I have ______ to say about the project — it was perfect.
______ in the class enjoyed the music performance.
There is ______ to worry about — we are all prepared.
Does ______ have a spare paintbrush I can borrow?
______ was at the gallery when I arrived — it was completely empty.
______ was covered in paint after the art lesson!

grammar.title: Indefinite Pronouns — Advanced Uses
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Now that you know the four groups, let us look at some more advanced rules. These will help you use indefinite pronouns like a confident English speaker.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Now that you know the four groups, let us look at some more advanced rules. These will help you use indefinite pronouns like a confident English speaker.
Using they/their/them with indefinite pronouns
In modern English, we often use they, their, and them to refer back to a singular indefinite pronoun when we do not know or do not want to specify the person's gender:
"Someone left their bag on the chair."
"Everyone should bring their own notebook."
"If anyone needs help, they can ask the teacher."
This is now accepted in standard English. It avoids the awkwardness of writing "his or her" every time.
Indefinite pronouns with adjectives
When an adjective describes an indefinite pronoun, the adjective comes after the pronoun (not before):
"I want to eat something delicious." (not "delicious something")
"Is there anywhere quiet where I can study?" (not "quiet anywhere")
"We need someone brave to speak first." (not "brave someone")
Indefinite pronouns with else
You can add else after an indefinite pronoun to mean "another" or "different":
"Is there anything else you need?"
"Someone else can take the lead today."
"Let us go somewhere else — this park is too crowded."
Double negatives — a trap to avoid
In English, we do not use two negative words in the same clause:
Mistake: "I do not have nothing." ✗ (Two negatives cancel out.)
Fix: "I do not have anything." ✓   OR   "I have nothing." ✓
Practice 2
Choose the correct option in each sentence:
I need (something cold / cold something) to drink on this hot day.
If (anyone / someone) calls, tell them I am busy.
(Everyone / No one) brought (their / his) art supplies today, so we are all ready.
She does not want (anything / nothing) for her birthday.
Let us try (somewhere else / else somewhere) for dinner tonight.
There is (anyone / no one) in the classroom — it is completely empty.
(Somebody / Anybody) should clean up the paint spills before we leave.
I have not seen (anyone / someone) I know at this exhibition.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Adjective Order
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
In English, when we use more than one adjective before a noun, there is a natural order. If we put the adjectives in the wrong order, the sentence sounds odd to native speakers — even if the grammar is technically correct. Learning this order will make your writing sound polished and natural.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
In English, when we use more than one adjective before a noun, there is a natural order. If we put the adjectives in the wrong order, the sentence sounds odd to native speakers — even if the grammar is technically correct. Learning this order will make your writing sound polished and natural.
The OSASCOMP order
Use the word OSASCOMP to remember the standard order:
O — Opinion: beautiful, ugly, amazing, terrible, lovely
S — Size: big, small, tiny, enormous, tall, short
A — Age: old, new, young, ancient, modern
S — Shape: round, square, flat, rectangular, circular
C — Colour: red, blue, green, bright, dark, colourful
O — Origin: Somali, East African, British, Chinese, Kenyan
M — Material: wooden, metal, plastic, stone, glass, recycled
P — Purpose: cooking, sleeping, painting, writing, farming
Step-by-step guide
When you have several adjectives, follow these steps:
Identify each adjective and its category (opinion, size, age, etc.).
Place them in the OSASCOMP order.
Check: does the phrase sound natural when you say it out loud?
Examples
"a beautiful small old round bowl" — opinion + size + age + shape
"a large rectangular wooden board" — size + shape + material
"a lovely bright green Somali cloth" — opinion + colour + origin
"three tiny new circular metal trays" — quantity + size + age + shape + material
"a beautiful tall colourful East African scene" — opinion + size + colour + origin
A common mistake
Mistake: "a wooden large rectangular board" ✗ — material (wooden) came before size (large).
Fix: "a large rectangular wooden board" ✓ — size then shape then material.
Memory tip
Think: Opinion first, purpose last. What you think about the object goes at the very beginning, and what the object is used for goes at the end, just before the noun.
Practice 3
Put the adjectives in the correct order before the noun:
a ______ ______ ______ table (round / old / beautiful)
a ______ ______ ______ flag (Somali / large / blue)
______ ______ ______ drums (recycled / big / three)
a ______ ______ ______ mural (colourful / amazing / East African)
a ______ ______ ______ house (new / stone / small)
some ______ ______ ______ brushes (painting / long / wooden)

grammar.title: Present Perfect Tense (Review)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense connects the past with the present. It tells us about actions that happened at some point in the past but are still relevant now. In the story "The Children of Wind Valley," the characters use the present perfect to show what they have already accomplished:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense connects the past with the present. It tells us about actions that happened at some point in the past but are still relevant now. In the story "The Children of Wind Valley," the characters use the present perfect to show what they have already accomplished:
"We have measured changes in temperature."
"We have mapped the affected zones."
"We have spoken to local farmers."
How to form it
Subject + have / has + past participle.
I / you / we / they + have + past participle: "We have planted fifty trees."
he / she / it + has + past participle: "She has inspired many people."
Negative: add not after have/has: "They have not finished yet." (or "haven't")
Question: put have/has before the subject: "Have you seen the new mural?"
Regular vs. irregular past participles
Regular: add -ed to the base verb: plant → planted, measure → measured, clean → cleaned.
Irregular: these must be memorised: go → gone, see → seen, build → built, speak → spoken, write → written, give → given, take → taken, make → made.
When to use the present perfect
For experiences: "I have visited three countries."
For achievements: "She has written a beautiful poem."
For changes over time: "The neighbourhood has changed a lot."
For unfinished time: "We have planted 47 trees this term." (the term is not over yet)
A common mistake
Mistake: "They have flourish in the subtropics." ✗ (Forgot the past participle form.)
Fix: "They have flourished in the subtropics." ✓
Mistake: "She have completed her painting." ✗ (Wrong form of have.)
Fix: "She has completed her painting." ✓
Memory tip
Think of the present perfect as a bridge between the past and the present. "Have" is the bridge, and the past participle is the action that has crossed over to affect the now.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with the present perfect form of the verb in brackets:
She ______ (visit) the art gallery three times this year.
They ______ (complete) their group project.
We ______ (not see) the finished mural yet.
______ you ever ______ (build) something from recycled materials?
The garden ______ (grow) much bigger since last month.
I ______ (write) a poem about nature.
He ______ (not speak) to the teacher about his idea yet.
The school ______ (plant) over a hundred trees this year.

grammar.title: Putting It All Together
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
In real English writing and speaking, we combine many grammar features in the same text. This lesson helps you practise using indefinite pronouns, adjective order, and the present perfect tense together — just as you will in the Writing Workshop.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
In real English writing and speaking, we combine many grammar features in the same text. This lesson helps you practise using indefinite pronouns, adjective order, and the present perfect tense together — just as you will in the Writing Workshop.
Example paragraph
Read this paragraph and notice the grammar features:
"Our school has started a recycling project this term. Everyone in Year 6 has collected plastic bottles and old newspapers. Someone donated a large beautiful wooden box to store the materials. We have already made three small colourful recycled sculptures for the school garden. Nobody expected the project to grow so quickly. Now anyone who visits our school can see something amazing — art made from things that people once threw away."
In this paragraph, find:
Three indefinite pronouns and their group (some-, any-, every-, no-).
Two examples of adjective order (list the adjectives and their categories).
Two sentences using the present perfect tense.
Mixed Practice
Write your own paragraph (six to eight sentences) about an imaginary school project. Your paragraph must include:
At least three different indefinite pronouns.
At least two phrases with adjectives in the correct order.
At least two sentences using the present perfect tense.
At least three vocabulary words from Unit 9.

grammar.title: Grade 6 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.

writing.title: Writing 1: My Art Project Description
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will plan and write a detailed description of an art project. This could be a real project you have done, or one you imagine. The goal is to practise your vocabulary, grammar, and descriptive writing skills.
Step 1: Choose Your Project
Pick one of these art project ideas, or create your own:
A mural for a wall in your school or neighbourhood.
A set of musical instruments made from recycled materials.
A poetry and art exhibition about protecting the environment.
A community garden with painted signs and sculptures.
A photography project showing the beauty of your local landscape.
Step 2: Plan Your Description
Before you write, answer these planning questions in your notebook:
What is your project called?
Where will it take place? Describe the location.

quizzes.question: What does 'deforestation' mean?
quizzes.options: A type of plant with a strong, fresh smell and taste, often used in teas and sweets. | The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry. | The height of something above sea level or ground level. | The process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest or area of land.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest or area of land.
quizzes.explanation: Deforestation can harm animals that live in the trees and cause floods.

quizzes.question: What does 'landscape' mean?
quizzes.options: A machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy or power. | A type of plant with a strong, fresh smell and taste, often used in teas and sweets. | The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry. | The visible features of an area of land, including mountains, trees, and water.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The visible features of an area of land, including mountains, trees, and water.
quizzes.explanation: The landscape was beautiful, with tall mountains and green fields.

quizzes.question: What does 'altitude' mean?
quizzes.options: A machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy or power. | A type of plant with a strong, fresh smell and taste, often used in teas and sweets. | The height of something above sea level or ground level. | To make changes to something in order to improve it.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The height of something above sea level or ground level.
quizzes.explanation: The airplane flew at a high altitude, far above the clouds.

quizzes.question: What does 'subtropics' mean?
quizzes.options: A machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy or power. | The act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause. | The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry. | To make changes to something in order to improve it.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry.
quizzes.explanation: Many plants that grow in the subtropics need lots of sunshine and warm temperatures.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest or area of land. Deforestation can harm animals that live in the trees and cause floods.
The visible features of an area of land, including mountains, trees, and water. The landscape was beautiful, with tall mountains and green fields.
The height of something above sea level or ground level. The airplane flew at a high altitude, far above the clouds.
The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry. Many plants that grow in the subtropics need lots of sunshine and warm temperatures.
A type of plant with a strong, fresh smell and taste, often used in teas and sweets. I love to drink peppermint tea when I feel cold.
A machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy or power. The wind turbine on the hill helps to produce electricity for our town.
To make changes to something in order to improve it. The school wants to reform the rules to make them fairer for everyone.
The act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause. Many young people join activism to help protect the environment.
To keep something in its original state or condition so it is not damaged or spoiled. The scientists preserved the ancient artefacts in special containers.
Giving up something important or valuable for the sake of something else. She made a sacrifice by giving her lunch to a friend who forgot theirs.
